sections.heading: What is Retrofit?
sections.content: Retrofit is a type-safe HTTP client for Android developed by Square. It turns your REST API into a Kotlin interface — you define your API endpoints as annotated functions, and Retrofit generates all the networking code automatically. Instead of manually building HTTP requests, parsing responses, and managing threads, you simply call a function and Retrofit handles everything: the HTTP request construction, the network call via OkHttp, the JSON parsing via a converter (Gson, Moshi, or kotlinx.serialization), and the response delivery. Retrofit is the industry standard for Android networking and is used in virtually every production Android app.

sections.heading: Why Retrofit?
items: Type-safe — API endpoints are Kotlin functions with typed parameters and return types; wrong types cause compile errors
Coroutine support — suspend functions make async network calls look like synchronous code
Automatic JSON parsing — pluggable converters (Gson, Moshi) parse response JSON into Kotlin data classes
OkHttp integration — built on top of OkHttp for connection pooling, caching, interceptors, and timeouts
Annotation-driven — HTTP method, URL, headers, query params, and body are all declared as annotations
Error handling — non-2xx responses throw HttpException; network failures throw IOException
Flexible — supports multipart uploads, streaming, custom converters, and dynamic URLs
Widely adopted — extensive community support, documentation, and library ecosystem

sections.heading: Retrofit vs Manual HttpURLConnection vs Volley

rows.retrofit: Annotated Kotlin interface — clean and declarative
rows.httpurlconnection: Manual — open connection, set method, write body, read stream
rows.volley: Request objects — more abstraction than HttpURLConnection but less than Retrofit
Built-in basic JSON support; limited type safety

rows.retrofit: Full OkHttp interceptor chain — headers, logging, auth, caching
rows.httpurlconnection: Manual — set headers on each connection

rows.retrofit: Minimal — define interface, Retrofit does the rest
rows.httpurlconnection: Very high — dozens of lines per request

rows.retrofit: Industry standard — use for all Android networking
rows.volley: Legacy — prefer Retrofit for new projects

sections.heading: Setting Up Retrofit
sections.content: To use Retrofit, add the Retrofit dependency along with the OkHttp dependency and a JSON converter to your app-level build.gradle.kts. The most common converter is Gson for its simplicity, though Moshi and kotlinx.serialization are excellent alternatives with better Kotlin support. You also optionally add the OkHttp logging interceptor for debugging network requests during development.

sections.heading: Retrofit Setup Steps
items: Step 1: Add Retrofit dependency: implementation("com.squareup.retrofit2:retrofit:2.11.x")
Step 2: Add Gson converter: implementation("com.squareup.retrofit2:converter-gson:2.11.x")
Step 3: Add OkHttp logging interceptor for debug: implementation("com.squareup.okhttp3:logging-interceptor:4.12.x")
Step 4: Add INTERNET permission to AndroidManifest.xml: <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" />
Step 5: Create your API service interface with annotated suspend functions
Step 6: Create Kotlin data classes matching your API's JSON response structure
Step 7: Build a Retrofit instance using Retrofit.Builder() with base URL and converter
Step 8: Create the API service implementation: retrofit.create(UserApiService::class.java)
Step 9: Provide Retrofit and the API service as singletons via Hilt for dependency injection

rows.description: Defines a GET request to the specified endpoint path
Defines a POST request — typically used to create a new resource
rows.example: @POST("users") suspend fun createUser(@Body user: CreateUserRequest): User

rows.description: Defines a PUT request — replaces an entire resource
rows.example: @PUT("users/{id}") suspend fun updateUser(@Path("id") id: Int, @Body user: User): User

rows.description: Defines a PATCH request — partially updates a resource
rows.example: @PATCH("users/{id}") suspend fun patchUser(@Path("id") id: Int, @Body updates: Map<String, Any>): User

rows.description: Defines a DELETE request — removes a resource
rows.example: @DELETE("users/{id}") suspend fun deleteUser(@Path("id") id: Int): Response<Unit>

rows.description: Defines a HEAD request — retrieves headers only, no body
rows.example: @HEAD("users/{id}") suspend fun checkUser(@Path("id") id: Int): Response<Void>

rows.description: Custom HTTP method for non-standard verbs
rows.example: @HTTP(method = "PURGE", path = "cache", hasBody = false) suspend fun clearCache()

sections.heading: Retrofit Parameter Annotations
items: @Path("id") — replaces a path variable in the URL: @GET("users/{id}") with @Path("id") id: Int → /users/42
@Query("page") — appends a query parameter: @GET("users") with @Query("page") page: Int → /users?page=2
@QueryMap — appends a Map<String, String> as multiple query parameters: @QueryMap options: Map<String, String>
@Body — sends a Kotlin object as the JSON request body (serialized by the converter): @Body user: CreateUserRequest
@Field and @FormUrlEncoded — sends form-encoded data instead of JSON body
@Part and @Multipart — sends multipart data for file uploads
@Header("Authorization") — adds a single dynamic header: @Header("Authorization") token: String
@Headers — adds static headers declared as strings: @Headers("Content-Type: application/json")
@Url — replaces the entire URL dynamically: suspend fun getFromUrl(@Url url: String): ResponseBody

rows.description: Returns the parsed data class directly — throws HttpException on non-2xx; simplest pattern
rows.use_case: When you only need the body and want automatic exception on error

rows.description: Returns a Retrofit Response wrapper with status code, headers, and body
rows.use_case: When you need to inspect the status code or headers alongside the body

rows.description: Returns the raw OkHttp ResponseBody — parse manually
rows.use_case: When working with non-JSON responses like plain text or binary data

rows.description: For operations with no response body (204 No Content)
rows.use_case: DELETE or POST operations that return no JSON body

rows.description: Alternative for no-body responses — equivalent to Response<Unit>
rows.use_case: Legacy pattern — prefer Response<Unit> in Kotlin

sections.heading: What is OkHttp?
sections.content: OkHttp is the HTTP client that powers Retrofit under the hood, developed by Square. While Retrofit handles the high-level API definition and JSON parsing, OkHttp handles the actual network communication — opening connections, managing connection pools, handling redirects, retrying failed requests, and processing TLS/SSL. OkHttp's most powerful feature for Android developers is its Interceptor system, which lets you inspect and modify every request and response that passes through the client — perfect for adding auth headers, logging, caching, and error handling in one place.

sections.heading: Building the OkHttpClient
items: Create with OkHttpClient.Builder() and configure it before passing to Retrofit
Set connect timeout: .connectTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
Set read timeout: .readTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
Set write timeout: .writeTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
Add logging interceptor for debug builds: .addInterceptor(HttpLoggingInterceptor().apply { level = HttpLoggingInterceptor.Level.BODY })
Add auth interceptor: .addInterceptor(AuthInterceptor(tokenProvider))
Add network interceptor for cache: .addNetworkInterceptor(cacheInterceptor)
Enable response caching: .cache(Cache(cacheDir, cacheSize))
Build: val client = OkHttpClient.Builder().connectTimeout(...).addInterceptor(...).build()

rows.called: Once per call — before any network activity
rows.use_for: Adding auth headers, request logging, retrying on 401

rows.called: Once per network request — after redirects and retries
rows.use_for: Cache headers, response body transformation, network-level logging

sections.heading: Writing a Custom Auth Interceptor
items: Create a class implementing okhttp3.Interceptor and override intercept(chain: Chain): Response
Get the original request: val original = chain.request()
Build a new request with the auth header: val request = original.newBuilder().header("Authorization", "Bearer $token").build()
Proceed with the modified request: return chain.proceed(request)
For token refresh: check if response is 401, refresh the token, then retry the request with the new token
Inject the TokenProvider or SessionManager into the interceptor via constructor
Add to OkHttpClient: .addInterceptor(AuthInterceptor(tokenProvider))
Auth interceptors added with addInterceptor() run before the network call — ideal for adding headers

rows.use_for: Production builds — no sensitive data logged

rows.logs: Request method, URL, response status code, and response size
rows.use_for: Lightweight logging — confirm requests are being made

rows.logs: Request and response headers in addition to BASIC info
rows.use_for: Debugging auth headers and response metadata

rows.logs: Full request and response including headers and body JSON
rows.use_for: Development and debugging — never in production (may log sensitive data)

sections.heading: Building the Retrofit Instance
sections.content: The Retrofit instance is the central configuration object that defines the base URL, the OkHttpClient (for networking), and the converter factory (for JSON parsing). It should be a singleton — created once and reused throughout the app. The Retrofit instance is used to create API service implementations by calling retrofit.create(YourApiService::class.java). With Hilt, you provide both the Retrofit instance and the API service as singletons in a @Module.

sections.heading: Retrofit.Builder Configuration
items: baseUrl() — the base URL all endpoint paths are relative to; must end with a trailing slash: "https://api.example.com/v1/"
client() — provides the configured OkHttpClient with interceptors and timeouts
addConverterFactory(GsonConverterFactory.create()) — adds Gson JSON parsing support
addConverterFactory(MoshiConverterFactory.create(moshi)) — alternative: Moshi with Kotlin support
addConverterFactory(Json.asConverterFactory("application/json".toMediaType())) — kotlinx.serialization
Multiple converter factories can be added — Retrofit tries them in order
build() — creates the Retrofit instance
retrofit.create(UserApiService::class.java) — generates the implementation of the API interface

rows.code_description: class UserRepository @Inject constructor(private val api: UserApiService, private val dao: UserDao)
suspend fun refresh() { val users = api.getUsers(); dao.insertAll(users.map { it.toEntity() }) }

sections.heading: Handling Retrofit Errors
items: Wrap Retrofit calls in try-catch in the Repository layer — never in the ViewModel
catch (e: HttpException) — server returned a non-2xx status code; e.code() gives the status code
catch (e: IOException) — network error: no connection, timeout, or DNS failure
Parse the error body: e.response()?.errorBody()?.string() — JSON string with error details from the server
Use Gson to parse error body: gson.fromJson(errorBody, ApiError::class.java)
Return Result.Success or Result.Error from the Repository to the ViewModel
For Response<T> return type: check response.isSuccessful before accessing response.body()
Never catch generic Exception without re-throwing or logging — silent failures are hard to debug

rows.description: @Multipart @POST("upload") suspend fun uploadFile(@Part file: MultipartBody.Part, @Part("description") description: RequestBody): UploadResponse

sections.heading: Retrofit with Response<T> — Handling Status Codes
items: Use Response<T> return type when you need to inspect status code or headers: suspend fun getUser(): Response<User>
Check success: if (response.isSuccessful) { val user = response.body() }
Get status code: response.code() — e.g., 200, 404, 500
Get error body on failure: response.errorBody()?.string()
Get response headers: response.headers()["X-Request-Id"]
response.body() is null on non-2xx responses — always check isSuccessful first
For 204 No Content: response.isSuccessful is true but response.body() is null — handle both cases
Using Response<T> instead of plain T gives you more control but adds more null checking

rows.kotlin_support: Basic — no native null safety or default values
rows.best_for: Simple projects; widely used; familiar to most Android developers

rows.kotlin_support: Excellent — Kotlin-aware; supports default values and null safety
rows.best_for: Kotlin-first projects needing better type safety than Gson

rows.kotlin_support: Native Kotlin — @Serializable annotation on data classes
rows.best_for: Kotlin Multiplatform projects; fully Kotlin ecosystem

sections.heading: Best Practices for Retrofit & OkHttp
items: Always create Retrofit and OkHttpClient as singletons — creating per request wastes resources and connections
Use OkHttp interceptors for cross-cutting concerns: auth headers, logging, analytics, retry logic
Never add the logging interceptor in release builds — it may log sensitive tokens and user data
Set reasonable timeouts — default OkHttp timeout is 10 seconds; adjust based on your API's performance
Keep the base URL in a constants file or BuildConfig — never hardcode it in the Retrofit builder
Use suspend functions for all API calls — eliminates callback hell and integrates naturally with coroutines
Handle both HttpException and IOException separately — they represent different failure modes
Test with MockWebServer — provides a real local HTTP server for integration testing of API calls

rows.mistake: Missing trailing slash in base URL
rows.fix: Base URL must end with /: "https://api.example.com/v1/" not "https://api.example.com/v1"

rows.mistake: Using absolute URL paths starting with / in endpoints
rows.fix: Endpoint paths should be relative ("users/{id}") not absolute ("/users/{id}") — absolute paths drop the base URL path

rows.mistake: Calling API service functions on the main thread without coroutines
rows.fix: Always call suspend API functions from viewModelScope.launch or a Repository's coroutine context

rows.mistake: Creating a new Retrofit instance per network call
rows.fix: Create once as a @Singleton via Hilt — reuse the same instance throughout the app

rows.mistake: Logging full request bodies in production
rows.fix: Only add HttpLoggingInterceptor in debug builds: if (BuildConfig.DEBUG) builder.addInterceptor(loggingInterceptor)

rows.mistake: Not handling non-2xx responses when using plain T return type
rows.fix: Wrap calls in try-catch for HttpException or use Response<T> to inspect status codes explicitly
Add <uses-permission android:name="android.permission.INTERNET" /> — without it all network calls fail silently

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Retrofit and OkHttp together form the definitive Android networking stack. Retrofit's annotation-driven API interface transforms your REST endpoints into clean Kotlin suspend functions with automatic JSON parsing, while OkHttp provides the robust transport layer with interceptors for auth, logging, and caching. Provide both as Hilt singletons, wrap all calls in Repository try-catch blocks with Result wrappers, and use the HttpLoggingInterceptor exclusively in debug builds. This combination delivers a networking layer that is clean, testable, performant, and production-ready.
